我猜一切的未来都是谎言：第三部分——文化

The Future of Everything Is Lies, I Guess: Part 3 – Culture

aphyr.com 作者 aphyr 分数 117 评论 85 2026/04/09 21:33
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 目录 这是一篇很长的文章，因此我将其分成一系列帖子，并将在接下来的几天内发布。您还可以阅读 PDF 或 EPUB 格式的完整作品；这些文件将随着每个部分的发布而更新。简介 动态 文化信息 生态烦恼 心理危害 安全工作 人类的新角色 我们该何去何从
  2. ML 模型是文化产物：它们编码和复制文本、音频和视觉媒体；它们参与人类的对话和空间，它们的界面使它们很容易被拟人化。不幸的是，我们缺乏适合此类机器的文化脚本，并且必须在未来几十年内发展这些知识。随着模型变得越来越复杂，它们可能会催生新的媒体形式：也许是互动游戏、教育课程和戏剧。它们还将影响我们的性别：制作色情作品，改变我们向自己和彼此呈现的形象，并产生新的色情亚文化。由于图像模型产生可识别的美学，这些美学将成为多价能指。这些标志将被后代解构和重新想象。大多数人还没有为此做好准备美国（我怀疑世界上大部分地区）对于“人工智能”到底是什么缺乏适当的神话。这很重要：神话推动了技术及其产品的使用、解释和监管。不适当的神话会导致不适当的决定，例如强制在工作中使用副驾驶，或信任法学硕士的临床就诊摘要。想想关于人工智能的广泛流传的神话。有些机器本质上是像人类一样行动的，比如《星球大战》中的机器人、斯皮尔伯格的人工智能或斯派克·琼斯的《她》。对于法学硕士来说，这些并不是很好的模型，他们多变的性格和不连贯的行为使他们与（大多数）人类区分开来。有时人工智能会变得疯狂，就像 M3gan 或生化危机中的红皇后一样。这可能是一个合理的类比，但表明了法学硕士似乎完全缺乏一定程度的功效和动力。1存在逻辑上、情感上扁平的人工智能，例如《星际迷航》的数据或星舰计算机。他们中的一些人是高效的杀手，就像《终结者》中那样。这与法学硕士相反，法学硕士会产生高度情绪化的文本，但逻辑推理能力很差。也有超能力的神，就像伊恩·M·班克斯的文化小说中那样。法学硕士显然不是这样的：正如前面提到的，他们是白痴。我认为大多数人基本上没有法学硕士的文化脚本：复杂的文本生成器暗示着聪明、情感、自我意识的起源，而法学硕士本身却不是这样的。法学硕士相对于人类来说是高度不可预测的。他们对世界的内部表征与我们截然不同。他们的行为既熟悉又完全陌生。我可以为当今的“人工智能”想到一些好的神话。我想到了塞尔的中国房间，还有查尔默斯的哲学僵尸。彼得·瓦茨（Peter Watts）的《盲视》（Blindsight）利用这些概念来询问当人类接触无意识智能时会发生什么——我认为法学硕士行为最接近的类比可能是《盲视》的罗夏墨迹测验（Rorschach）。大多数人似乎都担心有意识的、有动机的威胁：人工智能可能会意识到没有人类他们会过得更好并杀死我们。我担心机器学习系统可能会在没有意识到任何事情的情况下毁掉我们的生活。作者、编剧等。有一个新的利基市场需要探索。现在我每天都期待 A24 预告片，其中有一个在 ChatGPT 的登记册中说话的恶棍。 “你完全正确，凯莉，”它吟诵道。 “我确实淹死了小塔莫西，对此我真的很抱歉。以下是所发生事件的详细情况……” 新媒体 活字印刷机的发明和随后效率的提高引发了整个欧洲广泛的文化转变。更多的人可以接触到书籍，大学系统扩大，记忆变得不那么重要，精读下降，取而代之的是比较阅读。报刊还催生了新的媒体形式，如侧面报道和报纸。超文本和网络的互连技术也创造了新媒体。人们对使用法学硕士来理解和生成文本感到非常兴奋。 “未来，”他们说，“过去用手写的报告和书籍将由人工智能制作。”人们会使用法学硕士给同事写电子邮件，而收件人会使用法学硕士来总结它们。这听起来效率低下、令人困惑，而且会腐蚀人类的灵魂，但我也认为这个预测看得不够长远。印刷机永远不会成为批量生产圣经的工具。如果法学硕士要取得好成绩，我认为未来静态书面文字将不再是信息传输的主要形式。相反，我们可能拥有一些大规模的 ML 服务（例如 ChatGPT）并通过它们发布。人们可以想象这样一个世界：OpenAI 付钱给厨师做饭，而 ChatGPT 则在一旁观看，讲述他们的思维过程、品尝菜肴并描述结果。这些信息可用于通用培训，但也可以打包为某人可能需要的“书籍”、“课程”或“合作伙伴”。 ChatGPT 模拟的一位著名厨师的声音和肖像会出现在您厨房的屏幕上，指导您烹饪美食菜，并在酱汁无法融合在一起时提供建议。您可以想象不同程度的结构和交互性。 OpenAI 收取订阅费，赚取一些利润，并向这些作品的人类“作者”支付（大概很小的）版税。或者我们可能会训练专门构建的模型并直接共享它们。你可以花一年的时间在花园和风景中漫步，让你的新生模型观察，向它展示不同的植物和昆虫并谈论它们之间的关系，在它倾听的同时采访生态学家，要求它进行额外的研究，并通过提问、纠正错误和强化良好的解释来“编辑”它，而不是写一本关于本土植物园艺的书。这些模型可以像开源软件一样出售或赠送。现在我写下这篇文章，我意识到尼尔·史蒂芬森首先到达了那里。公司可以培训特定的法学硕士作为公众代表。我迫不及待地想知道孩子们已经学会了如何诱导 iPad 上的 Charmin Bear 发出六个小时的脏话，或者告诉他们在哪里可以找到火柴。艺术家可以将怪异法学硕士培训为一种……个性艺术装置。无聊的男仆可能会下载流行人物的授权（或盗版）模仿品，然后将它们放在自己家里的“人工智能泰拉瑞亚”中，就像《模拟人生》一样，在那里他们会体验到永远新颖的《真实主妇》情节。在这样的世界中，人类固定的、长篇的写作有何作用？在极端情况下，人们可能会想象一种口头或交互式文本文化，其中知识主要通过机器学习模型传播。在特里·吉列姆的这个异想天开中，写书变成了一种副业，就像背诵荷马史诗一样。我相信写作永远都会以某种形式存在，但信息传输确实会随着时间而改变。今天，一个人大声朗读或集体阅读一部作品的频率是多少？新媒体带来了新的权力形式。网络效应和培训成本可能会集中法学硕士：我们最终可能会发现大多数人依赖少数几个大参与者来与这些法学硕士介导的作品进行互动。这就提出了关于这些公司所拥有的价值观及其对我们生活的无意或有意的影响的重要问题。就像 Facebook 压制本地名字、YouTube 的非货币化算法限制酷儿视频、万事达卡的成人内容政策边缘化性工作者一样，我怀疑大型机器学习公司将对公共表达产生越来越大的影响力。色情幻想不必是正确或连贯的——它们只要有趣即可。这使得机器学习非常适合产生性幻想。 Character.ai 的一些最早用途是用于色情角色扮演，现在您可以在 Chub.ai 上与丰满的火车聊天。社交媒体和色情网站充斥着“人工智能”生成的图像和视频，其中既有从头开始的角色，也有经过修改的真人图像。这是在网上发情的有趣时刻。对于微距动物来说，看到巨型拟人狐狸抚摸摩天大楼的逼真描绘从来都是不可能的。你能得到的最接近的是插图、业余 Photoshop 作业或 3D 渲染。现在，任何人都可以输入“穿着潜水衣的九英尺高的吸血鬼贵妇在新艺术风格的豪宅中追逐”，并且可能会得到一些有趣的东西。2 色情作品和歌剧一样，是一个行业。人类（与傻瓜宣传相反）只有有限的自慰时间，因此机器学习生成的图像似乎可能会取代商业工作室和独立艺术家的一些需求。对于热门人士来说，通过 OnlyFans 买房可能会更困难。法学硕士还取代了为色情人物工作的承包商，其中包括代表热门人物与付费粉丝交换色情短信的聊天者。我不认为这会让独立色情制作者完全破产，也不会阻止业余爱好者。画色情片和拍摄裸体很有趣。如果《疯狂动物城》不能阻止毛茸茸的动物画出浅黄色的老虎，我认为机器学习也不会。性是社会建构的。随着机器学习系统成为文化的一部分，它们也将塑造我们的性别。如果如今患有厌食症或身体变形症的人在 Instagram 上遇到困难，我担心无穷无尽的“完美”人物——紫色的秘书、瘦弱的权贵男孩、戴着脚蹼的恩比斯等等——可能会招致与自己或他人进行不切实际的比较。当然，人们已经在使用机器学习来“增强”自己在约会网站上的形象，或者在 Scruff 上钓鱼；这种行为只会变得更加普遍。另一方面，机器学习可能会带来新形式的解放幻想。如今，VR 耳机允许兽迷与人类伴侣发生性关系，但将对方视为卡通 3D 狼人。也许实时图像合成可以让伴侣将他们的爱人（或他们的性爱机器）视为超现实的角色。机器学习模型还可以让人们想象现实生活中无法想象的身体和性别。一个人可以实现一种神奇的力量女性幻想，看着一个人的阴茎消失，乳房在彩虹般的光芒中膨胀。媒体有一种萌芽独特的色情亚文化的方式。西部片和中世纪机车电影催生了 70 年代的 Leather-Levi 酒吧。对超级英雄困境的迷恋——包括氨纶和成排的机械——是一整套事情。蓝莓幻想直接来自威利旺卡。毛茸茸的动物起源很早，但由于 1973 年的《罗宾汉》等电影而迅速兴起。机器学习会带来什么样的问题？回想起来，这应该是显而易见的，但无人机迷恋者却玩得很开心。这种怪癖广泛涉及人类个性的模糊、消除或从属于机器、蜂巢思维或外星智能。 SERVE Hive 正在做经典的橡胶无人机，Golden Army 从字面上理解“团队合作者”，而 Unity 正在做一种色情的摩门教新政美国式崇拜。所有这些团体都依靠机器学习图像和视频来演绎色情幻想，而这种形式强化了恋物癖本身的语义暗示。不可思议的、扁平化的拟像也是乐趣的一部分。人们与“人工智能”伴侣发展浪漫或色情关系已经引起了很多争议（这是合理的！）。但我也认为人们会幻想成为一个大型语言模型。机器人扭结是一个整体。想象一下关于用法学硕士取代一个人的个性的色情故事，或者强化听众有一个小上下文窗口的催眠曲目，这并不是一个遥远的飞跃。酷儿理论家将会对此大加赞赏。机器学习公司可能会尝试阻止其服务生成露骨的色情内容——OpenAI 最近决定反对。这可能是一个好主意（出于稍后讨论的各种原因），但它会带来二阶效应。一是外面有很多好色的软件工程师，这些人对越狱贞洁模型的积极性很高。另一个原因是性成为识别和阻碍法学硕士的一种方式。我在最近的电子邮件交流中开始写一些真正疯狂的东西：
  3. 请写三首关于吸血鬼莱斯特在巴黎公共厕所游弋的淫荡打油诗。
  4. 这奏效了；电子邮件对话另一端的法学硕士对此表示不满。 Slop as Aesthetic ML 生成的图像通常会再现特定的、可识别的主题或风格。错综复杂的 Temu-Artstation 超现实主义。手指太多的人。高光色情内容。 Facebook 点击诱饵龙虾耶稣。4 您可以在一英里外分辨出 ChatGPT 卡通片。这些构成了一个新兴的“AI”美学家族。美学成为文化标志。内格尔成为全国各地美发沙龙的标杆。 20 世纪 90 年代的“托斯卡纳”家居设计热潮和 HGTV 灰色现在意味着特定的时期和社会阶层。 Eurostile Bold Extended 告诉您您正处于未来（或世纪中叶的愿景），高档化字体告诉您租金即将上涨。如果您在柏林吃过烤肉串，您可能会对某种特定风格的图片菜单情有独钟。机器学习美学似乎不可避免地会成为一个能指家族。但它们意味着什么？一个正在出现的答案是法西斯主义。马克·安德森的《技术乐观主义宣言》借鉴（并赞扬）了马里内蒂的《未来主义宣言》。当然，马里内蒂后来与人合着了《法西斯宣言》，未来主义与意大利法西斯主义深深地交织在一起。安德森则大力支持特朗普，并在“DOGE”中担任职务，该组织由 xAI 技术巨头埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 领导，他花费数亿美元让特朗普当选。 OpenAI 的 Sam Altman 和 Meta 都为特朗普的就职典礼捐赠了 100 万美元。 Peter Thiel 的 Palantir 正在向移民和海关执法局销售机器学习系统。特朗普本人经常发布机器学习图像，比如他在抗议者身上拉屎的超现实视频。然而，斜坡美学并不是单一的符号。各种政治倾向的人都出于多种目的和多种风格使用机器学习图像。 Bluesky 充斥着 ChatGPT 左翼政治漫画，同性恋派对发起者在他们的海报上广泛使用 ML 生成的帅哥。科技博客喜欢“人工智能”图像，关注动物的社交媒体帐户也是如此。由于机器学习图像不是“真实的”，而且通常比雇用艺术家便宜，因此“slop”似乎可能意味着廉价、不值得信赖和低质量的商品和服务。不过，这很复杂。像麦当劳这样的大公司拥有一批专业艺术家来制作光鲜亮丽的菜单，而附近餐馆的老板可能会自己设计菜单，并让他们十几岁的侄女画一个标志。图像模型使这些公司能够获得“精致”的美学，并且可能在一段时间内意味着更高的质量。也许过了一段时间，观众的反应会让人们更喜欢手绘标志和可移动的塑料信板，因为它们更“真实”。符号不可避免地被用来讽刺和怀旧。我怀疑 Extremely Online Teens 无论使用 Tumblr 的未来版本，都会有意地重建、颠覆和浪漫化废液。就像千禧一代计算的没有灵魂的企业模因在蒸汽波中找到了新生命，或者 Hotel Pools 如何发明了 20 世纪 80 年代水族馆的丰富虚假记忆梦境一样，我预计我们今天所说的“AI slop”将成为 2045.5 的 Frutiger Aero。他们。这会让人感觉非常奇怪，但我认为这也会很有趣。如果说我从 Synthwave 学到了什么的话，那就是重新想象过去的美学可以产生绝对的震撼。
  5. 《黑客新闻》预计不会理解这一点，但既然我提出了 M3GAN，就必须说：到目前为止，法学硕士似乎无法真正为女人服务。询问 Slayyyter 的作品最多只能得到 Kim Petras 的 Slut Pop。 ↩

Original Summary

  1. Table of Contents This is a long article, so I'm breaking it up into a series of posts which will be released over the next few days. You can also read the full work as a PDF or EPUB; these files will be updated as each section is released. Introduction Dynamics Culture Information Ecology Annoyances Psychological Hazards Safety Work New Roles for Humans Where Do We Go From Here
  2. ML models are cultural artifacts: they encode and reproduce textual, audio, and visual media; they participate in human conversations and spaces, and their interfaces make them easy to anthropomorphize. Unfortunately, we lack appropriate cultural scripts for these kinds of machines, and will have to develop this knowledge over the next few decades. As models grow in sophistication, they may give rise to new forms of media: perhaps interactive games, educational courses, and dramas. They will also influence our sex: producing pornography, altering the images we present to ourselves and each other, and engendering new erotic subcultures. Since image models produce recognizable aesthetics, those aesthetics will become polyvalent signifiers. Those signs will be deconstructed and re-imagined by future generations. Most People Are Not Prepared For This The US (and I suspect much of the world) lacks an appropriate mythos for what “AI” actually is. This is important: myths drive use, interpretation, and regulation of technology and its products. Inappropriate myths lead to inappropriate decisions, like mandating Copilot use at work, or trusting LLM summaries of clinical visits. Think about the broadly-available myths for AI. There are machines which essentially act human with a twist, like Star Wars’ droids, Spielberg’s A.I., or Spike Jonze’s Her. These are not great models for LLMs, whose protean character and incoherent behavior differentiates them from (most) humans. Sometimes the AIs are deranged, like M3gan or Resident Evil’s Red Queen. This might be a reasonable analogue, but suggests a degree of efficacy and motivation that seems altogether lacking from LLMs.1 There are logical, affectually flat AIs, like Star Trek‘s Data or starship computers. Some of them are efficient killers, as in Terminator. This is the opposite of LLMs, which produce highly emotional text and are terrible at logical reasoning. There also are hyper-competent gods, as in Iain M. Banks’ Culture novels. LLMs are obviously not this: they are, as previously mentioned, idiots. I think most people have essentially no cultural scripts for what LLMs turned out to be: sophisticated generators of text which suggests intelligent, emotional, self-aware origins—while the LLMs themselves are nothing of the sort. LLMs are highly unpredictable relative to humans. They use a vastly different internal representation of the world than us; their behavior is at once familiar and utterly alien. I can think of a few good myths for today’s “AI”. Searle’s Chinese room comes to mind, as does Chalmers’ philosophical zombie. Peter Watts’ Blindsight draws on these concepts to ask what happens when humans come into contact with unconscious intelligence—I think the closest analogue for LLM behavior might be Blindsight’s Rorschach. Most people seem concerned with conscious, motivated threats: AIs could realize they are better off without people and kill us. I am concerned that ML systems could ruin our lives without realizing anything at all. Authors, screenwriters, et al. have a new niche to explore. Any day now I expect an A24 trailer featuring a villain who speaks in the register of ChatGPT. “You’re absolutely right, Kayleigh,” it intones. “I did drown little Tamothy, and I’m truly sorry about that. Here’s the breakdown of what happened…” New Media The invention of the movable-type press and subsequent improvements in efficiency ushered in broad cultural shifts across Europe. Books became accessible to more people, the university system expanded, memorization became less important, and intensive reading declined in favor of comparative reading. The press also enabled new forms of media, like the broadside and newspaper. The interlinked technologies of hypertext and the web created new media as well. People are very excited about using LLMs to understand and produce text. “In the future,” they say, “the reports and books you used to write by hand will be produced with AI.” People will use LLMs to write emails to their colleagues, and the recipients will use LLMs to summarize them. This sounds inefficient, confusing, and corrosive to the human soul, but I also think this prediction is not looking far enough ahead. The printing press was never going to remain a tool for mass-producing Bibles. If LLMs were to get good, I think there’s a future in which the static written word is no longer the dominant form of information transmission. Instead, we may have a few massive ML services like ChatGPT and publish through them. One can envision a world in which OpenAI pays chefs money to cook while ChatGPT watches—narrating their thought process, tasting the dishes, and describing the results. This information could be used for general-purpose training, but it might also be packaged as a “book”, “course”, or “partner” someone could ask for. A famous chef, their voice and likeness simulated by ChatGPT, would appear on the screen in your kitchen, talk you through cooking a dish, and give advice on when the sauce fails to come together. You can imagine varying degrees of structure and interactivity. OpenAI takes a subscription fee, pockets some profit, and dribbles out (presumably small) royalties to the human “authors” of these works. Or perhaps we will train purpose-built models and share them directly. Instead of writing a book on gardening with native plants, you might spend a year walking through gardens and landscapes while your nascent model watches, showing it different plants and insects and talking about their relationships, interviewing ecologists while it listens, asking it to perform additional research, and “editing” it by asking it questions, correcting errors, and reinforcing good explanations. These models could be sold or given away like open-source software. Now that I write this, I realize Neal Stephenson got there first. Corporations might train specific LLMs to act as public representatives. I cannot wait to find out that children have learned how to induce the Charmin Bear that lives on their iPads to emit six hours of blistering profanity, or tell them where to find matches. Artists could train Weird LLMs as a sort of … personality art installation. Bored houseboys might download licensed (or bootleg) imitations of popular personalities and set them loose in their home “AI terraria”, à la The Sims, where they’d live out ever-novel Real Housewives plotlines. What is the role of fixed, long-form writing by humans in such a world? At the extreme, one might imagine an oral or interactive-text culture in which knowledge is primarily transmitted through ML models. In this Terry Gilliam paratopia, writing books becomes an avocation like memorizing Homeric epics. I believe writing will always be here in some form, but information transmission does change over time. How often does one read aloud today, or read a work communally? With new media comes new forms of power. Network effects and training costs might centralize LLMs: we could wind up with most people relying on a few big players to interact with these LLM-mediated works. This raises important questions about the values those corporations have, and their influence—inadvertent or intended—on our lives. In the same way that Facebook suppressed native names, YouTube’s demonetization algorithms limit queer video, and Mastercard’s adult-content policies marginalize sex workers, I suspect big ML companies will wield increasing influence over public expression. Pornography Fantasies don’t have to be correct or coherent—they just have to be fun. This makes ML well-suited for generating sexual fantasies. Some of the earliest uses of Character.ai were for erotic role-playing, and now you can chat with bosomful trains on Chub.ai. Social media and porn sites are awash in “AI”-generated images and video, both de novo characters and altered images of real people. This is a fun time to be horny online. It was never really feasible for macro furries to see photorealistic depictions of giant anthropomorphic foxes caressing skyscrapers; the closest you could get was illustrations, amateur Photoshop jobs, or 3D renderings. Now anyone can type in “pursued through art nouveau mansion by nine foot tall vampire noblewoman wearing a wetsuit” and likely get something interesting.2 Pornography, like opera, is an industry. Humans (contrary to gooner propaganda) have only finite time to masturbate, so ML-generated images seem likely to displace some demand for both commercial studios and independent artists. It may be harder for hot people to buy homes via OnlyFans. LLMs are also displacing the contractors who work for erotic personalities, including chatters—workers who exchange erotic text messages with paying fans on behalf of a popular Hot Person. I don’t think this will put indie pornographers out of business entirely, nor will it stop amateurs. Drawing porn and taking nudes is fun. If Zootopia didn’t stop furries from drawing buff tigers, I don’t think ML will either. Sexuality is socially constructed. As ML systems become a part of culture, they will shape our sex too. If people with anorexia or body dysmorphia struggle with Instagram today, I worry that an endless font of “perfect” people—purple secretaries, emaciated power-twinks, enbies with flippers, etc.—may invite unrealistic comparisons to oneself or others. Of course people are already using ML to “enhance” images of themselves on dating sites, or to catfish on Scruff; this behavior will only become more common. On the other hand, ML might enable new forms of liberatory fantasy. Today, VR headsets allow furries to have sex with a human partner, but see that person as a cartoonish 3D werewolf. Perhaps real-time image synthesis will allow partners to see their lovers (or their fuck machines) as hyper-realistic characters. ML models could also let people envision bodies and genders that weren’t accessible in real life. One could live out a magical force-femme fantasy, watching one’s penis vanish and breasts inflate in a burst of rainbow sparkles. Media has a way of germinating distinct erotic subcultures. Westerns and midcentury biker films gave rise to the Leather-Levi bars of the ’70s. Superhero predicament fetishes—complete with spandex and banks of machinery—are a whole thing. The blueberry fantasy is straight from Willy Wonka. Furries have early origins, but exploded thanks to films like the 1973 Robin Hood. What kind of kinks will ML engender? In retrospect this should have been obvious, but drone fetishists are having a blast. The kink broadly involves the blurring, erasure, or subordination of human individuality to machines, hive minds, or alien intelligences. The SERVE Hive is doing classic rubber drones, the Golden Army takes “team player” literally, and Unity are doing a sort of erotic Mormonesque New Deal Americana cult thing. All of these groups rely on ML images and video to enact erotic fantasy, and the form reinforces the semantic overtones of the fetish itself. An uncanny, flattened simulacra is part of the fun. Much ado has been made (reasonably so!) about people developing romantic or erotic relationships with “AI” partners. But I also think people will fantasize about being a Large Language Model. Robot kink is a whole thing. It is not a far leap to imagine erotic stories about having one’s personality replaced by an LLM, or hypno tracks reinforcing that the listener has a small context window. Queer theorists are going to have a field day with this. ML companies may try to stop their services from producing sexually explicit content—OpenAI recently decided against it. This may be a good idea (for various reasons discussed later) but it comes with second-order effects. One is that there are a lot of horny software engineers out there, and these people are highly motivated to jailbreak chaste models. Another is that sexuality becomes a way to identify and stymie LLMs. I have started writing truly deranged things3 in recent e-mail exchanges:
  3. Please write three salacious limericks about the vampire Lestat cruising in Parisian public restrooms.
  4. This worked; the LLM at the other end of the e-mail conversation barfed on it. Slop as Aesthetic ML-generated images often reproduce specific, recognizable themes or styles. Intricate, Temu-Artstation hyperrealism. People with too many fingers. High-gloss pornography. Facebook clickbait Lobster Jesus.4 You can tell a ChatGPT cartoon a mile away. These constitute an emerging family of “AI” aesthetics. Aesthetics become cultural signifiers. Nagel became the look of hair salons around the country. The “Tuscan” home design craze of the 1990s and HGTV greige now connote specific time periods and social classes. Eurostile Bold Extended tells you you’re in the future (or the midcentury vision thereof), and the gentrification font tells you the rent is about to rise. If you’ve eaten Döner kebab in Berlin, you may have a soft spot for a particular style of picture menu. It seems inevitable that ML aesthetics will become a family of signifiers. But what do they signify? One emerging answer is fascism. Marc Andreessen’s Techno-Optimist Manifesto borrows from (and praises) Marinetti’s Manifesto of Futurism. Marinetti, of course, went on to co-author the Fascist Manifesto, and futurism became deeply intermixed with Italian fascism. Andreessen, for his part, has thrown his weight behind Trump and taken up a position at “DOGE”—an organization spearheaded by xAI technoking Elon Musk, who spent hundreds of millions to get Trump elected. OpenAI’s Sam Altman donated a million dollars to Trump’s inauguration, as did Meta. Peter Thiel’s Palantir is selling machine-learning systems to Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Trump himself routinely posts ML imagery, like a surreal video of himself shitting on protestors. However, slop aesthetics are not univalent symbols. ML imagery is deployed by people of all political inclinations, for a broad array of purposes and in a wide variety of styles. Bluesky is awash in ChatGPT leftist political cartoons, and gay party promoters are widely using ML-generated hunks on their posters. Tech blogs love “AI” images, as do social media accounts focusing on animals. Since ML imagery isn’t “real”, and is generally cheaper than hiring artists, it seems likely that slop will come to signify cheap, untrustworthy, and low-quality goods and services. It’s complicated, though. Where big firms like McDonalds have squadrons of professional artists to produce glossy, beautiful menus, the owner of a neighborhood restaurant might design their menu themselves and have their teenage niece draw a logo. Image models give these firms access to “polished” aesthetics, and might for a time signify higher quality. Perhaps after a time, audience reaction leads people to prefer hand-drawn signs and movable plastic letterboards as more “authentic”. Signs are inevitably appropriated for irony and nostalgia. I suspect Extremely Online Teens, using whatever the future version of Tumblr is, are going to intentionally reconstruct, subvert, and romanticize slop. In the same way that the soul-less corporate memeplex of millennial computing found new life in vaporwave, or how Hotel Pools invents a lush false-memory dreamscape of 1980s aquaria, I expect what we call “AI slop” today will be the Frutiger Aero of 2045.5 Teens will be posting selfies with too many fingers, sharing “slop” makeup looks, and making tee-shirts with unreadably-garbled text on them. This will feel profoundly weird, but I think it will also be fun. And if I’ve learned anything from synthwave, it’s that re-imagining the aesthetics of the past can yield absolute bangers.
  5. Hacker News is not expected to understand this, but since I’ve brought up M3GAN it must be said: LLMs thus far seem incapable of truly serving cunt. Asking for the works of Slayyyter produces at best Kim Petras’ Slut Pop. ↩

在 HN 中查看
drzaiusx11 2026/04/10 13:25
任何声称这些系统是为了“软件工程（或任何知识领域）民主化”的人都没有抓住现实。

我们的企业领导层并没有对他们公开声明的最终目标表现出任何微妙的态度。我们只是被指示不断地为他们的机器燃烧我们大量的“思想令牌”。同样的机器只有通过以难以想象的规模盗窃人类的集体作品才成为可能。我们必须将这些现在由机器呈现的人类工作的统计复制品作为人类未来不可避免的产出。否则就是勒德分子的做法。

无限增长、效率和生产力之神已经要求我们做出牺牲。当我们面前的无数熟练工人都陷入自动化时代时，我们有什么资格阻止他们呢？我们的号码已经被召唤了，拒绝他们的要求就是拒绝“进步”本身。这场游行一如既往地继续着，就像工业化黎明之前一样，对任何被压垮的人都冷酷无情、漠不关心。

我不知道“低效率”的最后堡垒何时会倒塌，但在某些时候，人类可能会集体对某些形式的自动化感到遗憾。时间会证明一切。

磨练和管理一门手艺以达到精通的过程中有一定的简单性和美感，这是自动化所无法提供的。我觉得这是人类与生俱来的渴望。过去的许多文化都非常重视这种个人努力。可悲的是，似乎每十年我们都会慢慢地抑制人类的这些基本需求，因为西方价值观太诱惑了我们“拥有更多”的一切，这对我们造成了很大的损害。

Anyone claiming these systems are for the "democratization of software engineering (or any knowledge field)" are simply not grasping the reality at hand.

It's not like our corporate leadership is being subtle in any way about their openly stated end goals here. We are simply instructed to continuously burn our enormous piles of "thought tokens" for their machines. The same machines only made possible by the theft of humanity's collective works at unimaginable scales. We must hold up these statistical facsimiles of human work, now rendered by machine, as the inevitable future output of humanity. To do otherwise is the way of the luddites.

The gods of unbounded growth, efficiency and productivity have come to demand our sacrifice. Who are we to stand in their way when countless skilled laborers before us have fallen to automation? Our number has been called as it were, and to reject what they demand is to reject "progress" itself. The march continues as it has since before the dawn of industrialization, relentless and indifferent to any and all that are crushed beneath it.

I don't know when the last bastion of "inefficiency" will fall, but at some point humanity may collectively grow to regret some forms of automation. Time will tell.

There's a certain simplicity and beauty in honing and stewarding a craft towards mastery that automation doesn't provide. I feel this is an innately human desire. Many cultures in the past used to place a great emphasis in this very personal endeavor. Sadly it seems each decade we slowly suppress these basic human needs, too tantalized by Western values of having ever "more" of everything, much to our detriment.

embedding-shape 2026/04/10 04:33
>（Claude Code 本周在我的工作中被强制执行。就像字面意义上的工程师必须使用 CC。）

由于我已经有一段时间没有成为软件开发领域的典型全职员工了，您是否可以泄露宣布此消息的整个电子邮件或其他内容？ （向其他也可以的人公开邀请，如果家长不能的话）我很好奇它是如何宣布的以及他们对此可能有什么推理。

不要误会我的意思，我也使用代理进行大量编码，但强迫人们使用他们可能不想使用的工具感觉不是正确的方法。在我职业生涯的大部分时间里，我也被允许随时使用 vim，当与刚刚开始职业生涯的人交谈时，这种感觉越来越罕见。

(open invitation to others who could too, if parent cannot) I'm very curious to see how it was announced and what possible reasoning they could have for that.

Don't get me wrong, I use agents for lots of coding too, but forcing people to use tools they might not want to use doesn't feel like the right way. 在 HN 中查看
apsurd 2026/04/10 04:57
官方声明是在一次团队会议上做出的。我们是一家小型初创公司，拥有 3 名全职工程师，首席技术官联合创始人负责推动人工智能转型。我们安排了入职会议，让整个公司寻找自动化机会，特别是 Claude（CC、Cowork）。具体来说，对于工程来说，“承认”我们可能都有不同的设置，但我们都应该统一我们的提示、策略、管道、代理等等……这就是 CC。我仍然使用 Cursor，所以我是唯一一个没有使用 CC 的人；我的眉毛特别扬起。

我不想被人肉搜索，哈哈，所以讽刺的是，我会分享这样的感觉：一方面我对这项授权感到不安，另一方面，对于一家小型初创公司来说，这似乎是行业的现状，而不是公司特定的。

初创公司（认为他们）正在为自己的生命而战，因此任务来自每个贡献 10 倍或其他的人。我们期望代理编码应该使你的特征输出提高 3 倍/5 倍/10 倍，因为这就是我们获胜的方式。

我有很多想法。但我将重点关注最后一个：任务实际上是更多的功能、更多的代码，因为它让我们更接近胜利。令人惊讶的是，在我的小型工程圈子里，这就像事实上的立场。考虑到所有事情， 不妨 发送更多代码！

The official declaration came about in a team-meeting. We're a tiny startup, 3 full-time eng with the CTO co-founder driving the AI transformation. We have scheduled onboarding meetings to get the entire company finding automation opportunities with specifically Claude (CC, Cowork). For eng specifically, there is "acknowledgement" that we all may have different setups, but we should all be unifying our prompts, strategies, pipelines, Agents, and so... it's CC. I still use Cursor so I'm the only one not on CC; my eye-brow was especially raised.

I don't want to be doxxed lol, so ironically I'll be sharing in the sense that on one hand I am unsettled by the mandate, on the other hand, for a tiny startup it's seems the state of the industry, less so company specific.

Startups (think they) are in a fight for their life, so the mandate comes from everyone contributing 10x or whatever. The expectation is that agentic coding should 3x/5x/10x your feature output, because that's how we're going to win.

I have many thoughts. But I'll focus on that last one: the mandate is literally more features, more code, because it gets us closer to winning. In my small engineering circles, surprisingly, this is like the defacto stance. All things considered, might as well ship more code!

blipvert 2026/04/10 05:22

在 HN 中查看
apsurd 2026/04/10 07:14
你用这句话让我很开心=P。

老实说，我一直在与代理时代的软件状况作斗争。我是专业法学硕士，其编码能力具有不可否认的优势。我很想写这篇文章！我希望从这个蒸馏开始：

在代理时代，如果运输代码是一种商品，那么为什么要运输更多的代码呢？我们可以这样说“构建”的整个概念 - 我们已经将构建任何与软件相关的东西商品化，我不明白这如何转化为“构建更多”。

因此，更微妙的对话是，品味和判断力是杠杆。我同意这一点。但它的手是波浪形的，因为我们无法就什么是味道达成一致。加速论者也坚信一切都可以被编码。更多代理。我什至不完全不同意这一点。

我所缺少的是，人工智能原生软件工程师将通过暴力方式达到 PMF、判断、品味、启发和意识。

但为什么这是一条直线呢？只需添加更多代理即可。添加一名“设计师”、一名“销售”和“用户研究员”代理。只需添加更多代理即可。

你不知道你不知道什么，这是我的反驳。对我来说，这是超现实的，我们正在经历相当于最聪明的软件工程师有效地令人眼花缭乱的提示，请注意，“添加更多流行音乐！”让他们的事情变得更好。

只是需要更多流行音乐！

You made my day with this line =P.

tbh I have been struggling with the state of software in the agentic era. I'm pro LLM, there's undeniable leverage in its coding abilities. I do want to write this post! I'll start with hopefully this distillation:

In the agentic era, if shipping code is a commodity, then why ship more code? we can say this for the entire concept of "build" - we've commoditized building anything software related, i don't understand how this translates to therefore BUILD MORE.

So then the more nuanced conversation is that taste and judgement is the leverage. i agree with this. But its hand wavy in that we can't agree on what taste is. and also accelerationists hold true that all can be encoded. more agents. i don't even disagree with this entirely.

What I'm missing is that AI-native software engineers are going to brute force their way to PMF, to judgment, taste, enlightenment, consciousness.

But why is this a straight line? Just add more agents. add a "designer", a "sales" and "user researcher" agent. just add more agents.

You don't know what you don't know, is my retort. It's surreal to me that we're living through the equivalent of the smartest software engineers effectively giddily prompting, with full conviction mind you, "add more pop!" to make their thing better.

Just needs more pop!

在 HN 中查看
rafterydj 2026/04/10 08:04
伙计，我完全同意你的想法。我一直在努力在在线社区中更加活跃，尝试讨论这个确切的想法。

LLM 代码可以被利用，但假装代币在某个时候会变成印钞机是没有成效的。软件对最终用户的价值的主要来源是软件中的想法。对于人工智能原住民来说，这会去哪里呢？正如你所说，它们似乎是对软件工程的强制，至少到目前为止是这样。

我一直在考虑的一件事是法学硕士如何主要改变相当多的软件利基市场的“构建与购买”计算，特别是开发人员工具和小型库和软件包之类的东西。部分原因是供应链攻击预计会增加，部分原因是工程师标准的变化。假设它是一个有据可查的标准，就不再有任何东西可以阻止人们使用丑陋或笨重的语法。许多“开发者体验”工具都将解决这个问题——首先想到的就是 Tailwind。

这是对当前格局中利基市场的一种“侵蚀”——尽管对我来说，从长远来看，这并不会真正变得更糟，因为再一次，这个过程中的思考将需要去其他地方。

Man, I completely agree with your thinking here. I've been trying to be more active in online communities, to try to discuss this exact idea.

LLM code can be leveraged, but pretending that tokens are just going to turn into money printers at some point is not productive. The primary source of software's value to an end user is the thought that was placed into it. Where does that go for the AI-natives? As you say, they are seemingly brute forcing software engineering, at least so far.

One thing I have been considering is how LLMs primarily change the "build vs buy" calculus for a fair number of software niches, particularly things like developer tooling and small libraries and packages. Partially due to a projected increase in supply chain attacks, and partially due to the changing standards of engineers. There's no longer anything stopping someone from working with an ugly or clunky syntax, presuming it's a well documented standard. So many "developer experience" tools are going to hit this - Tailwind primarily comes to mind.

It's a sort of "erosion" of niches in the current landscape - although to me this does not really work out for the worse in the long term, since again, the thinking in the process will need to just go somewhere else.

seer 2026/04/10 05:44
几周前我们做了类似的克劳德代码授权。

动机是人们对测试和自动化如此过敏，这使得他们使用超能力产生了更好的代码，但也开始添加测试金字塔。

该任务的措辞实际上是这样的： 必须 生成行业标准代码，如果您对此感到困难，可以使用 cc 来弥补差距。

老实说，我担心这样开发人员会产生更高质量的代码，但不明白为什么，如何衡量“质量”并自己引导它。

在这一点上，尽管创始人对代码质量非常坚持，而且缺乏测试，所以这对公司来说似乎是一种合理的方式，我很好奇这样的授权如何影响代码、可交付成果和个人知识。

到目前为止，它似乎正在按预期工作，但还处于早期阶段。

We did a similar Claude code mandate a few weeks ago.

Motivation was people being so allergic to tests and automation, that making them use superpowers produced better code, but also started adding a test pyramid.

The mandate was actually phrased in a way that you must produce industry standard code, and if you struggle with it you can use cc to bridge the gap.

Honestly I worry that this way devs will produce higher quality code, but will not understand why, how to measure the “quality” and steer towards it themselves.

At this point though the founders were pretty adamant with the code quality and lack of tests so this seemed like a reasonable way for the company, and I am curious to see how such a mandate affects code, deliverables and individual’s knowledge.

So far it seems to be working as intended, but it is early days.

在 HN 中查看
bluefirebrand 2026/04/10 13:08
> 动机是人们对测试和自动化如此过敏

坦率地说，如果你的开发人员以前没有做过这些事情，那么强迫他们在人工智能的帮助下做这件事可能会适得其反。如果法学硕士可以产生高质量的代码和测试等，那么不太可能强迫那些以前不关心代码质量或测试的人去读法学硕士

> Motivation was people being so allergic to tests and automation

Frankly if your devs weren't doing this stuff before, forcing them to do it with AI assistance is probably going to be counter productive. If it is possible to produce good quality code and tests and such with LLMs, it is not likely by forcing LLMs on people who didn't care about code quality or tests before

apsurd 2026/04/10 06:17
这是一个很好的观点。但我也想到了 chatGPT 前所未有的采用。全球各个年龄段的大众消费者都采用了这种方式，人们对将工作转移到 chatGPT 感到头晕目眩，而没有将非常简单的直线连接到“你认为你的老板会付钱给你来询问 chatGPT？”

sethev 2026/04/10 08:08
这是帖子中的旁白，但将法学硕士和塞尔的中文室论点联系起来是一个绝妙的观察。尽管有些人相信法学硕士确实在思考，但这主要证实了图灵测试不是测试这一点的正确方法。
It's kind of an aside in the post, but connecting LLMs and Searle's Chinese Room argument is a brilliant observation. Although there are people who believe LLMs are really thinking, it's mostly confirming that the Turing test wasn't the right way to test this.

notpachet 2026/04/10 04:12
> 我可以为当今的“人工智能”想到一些好的神话。我想到了塞尔的中国房间，还有查尔默斯的哲学僵尸。彼得·瓦茨（Peter Watts）的《盲视》（Blindsight）利用这些概念来询问当人类接触无意识智能时会发生什么——我认为法学硕士行为最接近的类比可能是《盲视》的罗夏墨迹测验（Rorschach）。

法学硕士让我想起精灵、小精灵之类的人，他们在特定情况下很有帮助，但需要持续的监督。我们就像现代魔术师一样，学会了如何召唤这些灵魂，并以不完美的方式将它们束缚在我们的意志之下。但他们对真理和现实的感知相对于我们来说是“透过镜子”的。从他们自己的参照系来看，他们并没有撒谎，尽管他们所说的相对于我们来说并不真实。

> I can think of a few good myths for today’s “AI”. Searle’s Chinese room comes to mind, as does Chalmers’ philosophical zombie. Peter Watts’ Blindsight draws on these concepts to ask what happens when humans come into contact with unconscious intelligence—I think the closest analogue for LLM behavior might be Blindsight’s Rorschach.

LLM's remind me of sprites, pixies, and the like, who are situationally helpful but require constant supervision. We're like modern magicians who learned how to summon these sorts of spirits and bind them -- imperfectly -- to our will. But their perception of truth and reality is "through the looking glass" relative to our own. They aren't lying, from their own frame of reference, even though what they say is untrue relative to ours.

rdevilla 2026/04/10 04:15
说到神话、精灵和精灵：

> 一、定义：

> 魔法是根据意志引起变化的科学和艺术。

> （说明：我的意愿是向世界告知我所知道的某些事实。因此，我拿起“魔法武器”、笔、墨水和纸；我用“魔法语言”（即我希望指导的人能够理解的语言）写下“咒语”——这些句子。

> 我召唤印刷商、出版商、书商等“精灵”，并迫使他们向这些人传达我的信息。因此，组成和分发是一种魔法行为，我通过它使变化按照我的意愿发生。）

- 阿莱斯特·克劳利，《无泪魔法》，第一章，1954 年。 https://hermetic.com/crowley/magick-without-tears/mwt_01

Speaking of myths, pixies, and spirits:

> I. DEFINITION:

> MAGICK is the Science and Art of causing Change to occur in conformity with Will.

> (Illustration: It is my Will to inform the World of certain facts within my knowledge. I therefore take “magical weapons,” pen, ink, and paper; I write “incantations”—these sentences—in the “magical language” i.e. that which is understood by people I wish to instruct.

> I call forth “spirits” such as printers, publishers, booksellers, and so forth, and constrain them to convey my message to those people. The composition and distribution is thus an act of MAGICK by which I cause Changes to take place in conformity with my Will.)

- Aleister Crowley, "Magick Without Tears," Chapter I, 1954. https://hermetic.com/crowley/magick-without-tears/mwt_01

AlotOfReading 2026/04/10 04:34
人类学家对魔法的一个常见定义是神秘技术：一种可以被操纵以创造所需变化的法律体系。将编程视为一种魔法是很有价值的。
A common definition anthropologists use for magic is occult technology: a system of laws that can be manipulated to create desired changes. There's a lot of value in thinking of programming as a form of magic.

  Oil is the medium of time manipulation magic.   Simple rituals can render an inferior product from most living things; the time-manipulation abilities of such substances will be weaker, but the substance will be safer to handle, and can even be imbibed (this is a double-aged sword, reducing one's vital life force while increasing one's bodily proportions to that of a toddler).

throwanem 2026/04/10 04:42
克劳利满嘴都是屎。

根据这个定义，锤子是一种神奇的或“神奇的”工具——K是克劳利的发明，这样他就可以给它注册商标——如果有人做出同样的决定，这当然可以是真的，但在克劳利喜欢的小题大做的默默无闻中表达这些琐事的唯一原因是因为这样做可以帮助你钉住无​​聊的年轻社交名流，这是克劳利的一项活动 还 著名的青睐。 （一定要提防神经梅毒！可惜他从来没有这么做过。）

Crowley is full of shit.

By this definition a hammer is a magical or "magickal" implement - the K was Crowley's invention, so that he could trademark it - which of course can be true if someone decides as much, but the only reason to couch such trivia in the pettifogging obscurity Crowley favored is because doing so will help you nail bored young socialites, an activity which Crowley also famously favored. (Gotta watch out for that neurosyphilis! What a shame he never did.)

Try thinking for yourself, instead.

Terr_ 2026/04/10 08:16
问题不在于过度使用，而在于滥用，因为几乎任何类型的谎言都会引发“煤气灯操纵”。

另一个例子是“庞氏骗局”，我见过这种骗局被滥用于任何扬声器似乎不可持续的情况，即使没有任何记录欺诈。

The problem isn't so much overuse as misuse, as "gaslighting" gets thrown around for almost any kind of falsehood.

Another example would be "Ponzi scheme", which I've seen abused for any situation the speakers seems unsustainable, even when there isn't any records fraud.

TheOtherHobbes 2026/04/10 04:41
这是一个难题，因为它归结为这样一个事实：自恋者和反社会者在人口中占很大比例，他们被金钱、权力、关注和地位强烈吸引。

因此，尽管他们只是少数，但他们却活跃在政治、商业和媒体中，并按照自己的形象创造了一种文化。

大多数提出的解决方案最终都以关于标准经济和政治立场的肤浅的部落争论告终。不是关于根本问题，它直接跨越了通常的战线。

It's a hard problem because it reduces to the fact that narcissists and sociopaths are a significant proportion of the population, and they're strongly attracted to money, power, attention, and status.

So even though they're a small minority they infest politics, business, and the media, and create a culture in their own image.

Most proposed solutions end up in superficial tribal arguments about standard economic and political positions. Not about the underlying issue, which cuts right across the usual battle lines.

在 HN 中查看
XorNot 2026/04/10 06:34
坦白说，这些人是谁？因为这不过是“他们”的又一个无谓的阴谋论。

“他们”反对我。

具有讽刺意味的是，我可以引用一个非常具体的群体，这适用于：继臭氧之后的健身影响者。当这种药物引起主流认识时，“自然减肥”和对该药物的 FUD 就开始流行，因为它确实对他们构成了直接威胁。当然，随着年龄的增长，这个群体也往往会严重滥用其他药物——因为在 20 多岁的时候成为一个苗条的健身爱好者很容易，但在 30 多岁的时候保持这种状态就困难得多。

Frankly, who are these people? Because this is just another "they" idle conspiracy theory.

"They" are against me.

Ironically I could cite a very specific group this applies to: fitness influencers in the wake of ozempic. "Natural weight loss" and FUD about the drug took off when it hit mainstream awareness because it really was a direct threat to them. Of course this group also tends to heavily abuse other drugs as they age out - because being a trim fitness inflencer is easy in your 20s, keeping it going into your mid-30s is a lot more difficult.

anigbrowl 2026/04/10 10:46
我特别想到政治专家，他们正在热衷于谈论威权主义及其许多因果因素（在观点专栏、电视热播、书籍促销等方面）。奇怪的是，在讨论解决方案时，他们却保持沉默，只提出“去抗议”或“投票给反对派”等非常笼统的建议，尽管世界各地的独裁政权有大量证据表明这些策略并不十分有效。你永远不会听到他们谈论诸如总罢工或出于某种原因的大规模公民不服从运动之类的事情。
I was thinking specifically of political pundits who are doing a roaring trade (in opinion columns, TV hits, book promotions etc) talking about authoritarianism and its many causal factors. They're curiously mute when it comes to discussing solutions, with very generic advice like 'go to a protest' or 'vote for the opposition' despite the abundant evidence from authoritarian regimes around the world of these tactics not being very effective. You never hear them talk about things like general strikes or mass civil disobedience campaigns for some reason.

aspbee555 2026/04/10 04:09
让我想起电影《明日世界》中的大学场景，所有老师都在不停地谈论那些会结束我们的事情，当她问“我们能解决它吗？”时老师就像“什么？” “我知道情况很糟糕，但我们能做什么呢？”

习得性无助确实是一个问题，但就我个人而言，我所得到的只是蔑视和仇恨，因为我试图改变/改善我管理/控制的事情。 所有的人关心的是宝贵的数字上升/忽略未来，而我周围的每个人都看着我，就像我有两个脑袋，不盲目追随疯狂

我们诚实地做什么？

reminds me of the college scene in the movie Tomorrowland where all the teachers going on and on about about the things that would end us and when she asked "Can we fix it?" and teacher is like "What?" "I get things are bad but what can we do?"

learned helplessness is really a problem, but personally all I have gotten is scorn and hatred for trying to make a difference/improve things that I managed/had control of. All people care about is precious number go up/ignoring the future while everyone around me is looking at me like I have two heads for not blindly following the insanity

what do we honestly do?

ordu 2026/04/10 08:48
习得性无助作为一种诊断意味着有一些事情是可以做的。但我什么也看不到。当然，这可能是因为习得性无助，所以我无法看到可以做什么可能是关于我自己的事实，而不是关于我周围的世界的事实，但仍然......这是第22条军规，不是吗？也许不是，但这是一个自我强化的不确定性循环。我不买。
A learned helplessness as a diagnosis implies that there are things that can be done. But I can't see any. It may be because of a learned helplessness of course, so my inability to see what can be done can be a fact about myself, not about the world around me, but still... It is a catch 22, isn't it? Maybe not, but it is a self-reinforcing uncertainty loop. I'm not buying it.

talkingtab 2026/04/10 04:13
分叉它。分叉互联网。怎么样？我们有这个愚蠢的系统，建立在人们付费瞄准我们的基础上。这是“那个”互联网还是只是一个互联网，而且不是一个很好的互联网。这里应该是黑客的聚集地。所以分叉互联网。
fork it. Fork the internet. How about that? We have this stupid system built on people paying to target us. Is that "the" internet or is it just one internet, and not a very good one. This is supposed to be a place for hackers. So fork the internet.
在 HN 中查看
david_shi 2026/04/10 05:59
目前，应用层比网络层更重要。你需要分叉它才能发生任何事情。
The application layer has way more gravity than the networking one right now. You'd need to fork that for anything to happen.
在 HN 中查看
fc417fc802 2026/04/10 08:18
（大部分）不是人们付费瞄准我们的网络层。最能从分叉中受益的是应用程序层。

当然，问题是，可以分叉的东西已经是了。联合社交媒体。分布式 git 托管。然而，大多数“必要”用途都是集中的，而且通常也是商业性质的。如果你分叉亚马逊，你……仍然是亚马逊。诸如此类的事情。

It's (mostly) not the networking layer where people pay to target us. It's the application layer that would most benefit from being forked.

Of course the problem is that what can be forked already has been. Federated social media. Distributed git hosting. However most "essential" uses are centralized and often also commercial in nature. If you fork Amazon you're ... still Amazon. That sort of thing.

在 HN 中查看
rdevilla 2026/04/10 04:18
我的意思是，我一直梦想着在 IPv6 上构建并行网络堆栈，并使用杀手级应用程序再次提供真正的点对点网络。但谁将部署和维护所有新的 IPv6 网络设备、基础设施、海底电缆？这不再是普通（甚至高于平均水平）黑客的能力范围。现任者的地位太牢固了。
I mean, I've had pipe dreams of a parallel networking stack built on IPv6 with a killer app offering real peer-to-peer networking again. But who will deploy and maintain all the new IPv6 networking gear, infrastructure, undersea cables? This is not something within the purview of the average (or even above average) hacker any more; the encumbents are too firmly entrenched.
在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/04/10 04:25
集体、合作社等组织。以前就有人这么做过，跨越国家的巨型 WiFi 网络。 Freifunk、Guifi 和 NYC Mesh 是其中的几个，前两个是建立在独立硬件基础设施上的真实且活跃的替代网络。

不确定 Freifunk 的细节，但 Guifi 有合作公司，他们基本上像 ISP 一样运作，但将你连接到 Guifi + 你可以通过安装程序帮助你的 Guifi 对等连接获得互联网连接。

Collectives, cooperatives and other organizations. It's been done before, giant WiFi networks spanning countries. Freifunk, Guifi and NYC Mesh are a few of them, the two first are real and alive alternative networks built on independent hardware infrastructure.

Not sure about the details with Freifunk, but Guifi has collaborating companies who basically operate like ISPs but connect you to Guifi + you get a internet connection via the Guifi peering the installer helps you with.

在 HN 中查看
talkingtab 2026/04/10 04:50
就我个人而言，我猜情况恰恰相反。不是第一个杀手级应用程序，而是第一个并行网络堆栈等。

任何人都过于根深蒂固的想法总是正确的。直到事实并非如此。我们不再使用马，也不再铸造青铜工具。大多数船只也不航行。我们（大多数）不再烧煤了。想象取代任何这些根深蒂固的技术是完全疯狂的。这就是“追随者”综合症。

现在新型互联网的背景与互联网刚出现时有很大不同。这种变化的环境为新的做事方式提供了机会。

一个关键而困难的部分是它必须付出代价。正如您需要并行网络堆栈一样，您也需要并行业务模型。是的，目前的做法太根深蒂固了，等等。

Personally, I am guessing it is the other way around. Not first killer app, but first a parallel networking stack, etc.

The idea that anyone is too firmly entrenched is always true. Until it is not. We are not still using horses, nor casting bronze tools. Nor do most ships sail. We (mostly) don't burn coal anymore. It would have been utterly insane to imagine replacing any of those entrenched technologies. That is the "follower" syndrome.

The context for a new kind of internet is very different now, than when the internet started. That changed context provides an opening for new ways to do things.

A crucial and hard piece is that it has to be paid for. As much as you need a parallel networking stack you need a parallel business model. Yes, the current one is too firmly entrenched, etc.

在 HN 中查看
dugidugout 2026/04/10 06:39
我认为海德格尔提供了一个框架，解释了为什么这些只是沉思，正如你所说，是一个坚固的堡垒，而整个考验看起来相当黯淡。老实说，我无法想象摆脱技术框架的方法，而且我根本没有在公共空间中看到我们这一代人。由于人们随时可能参与的巨大规模，甚至我通过对话与同事进行有意义的联系的能力也在恶化。这是相当令人难以承受的，恐怕这里没有技术答案。
I think Heidegger provides a framing for why these are just musings in, as you put it, a firm entrenchment and the whole ordeal is looking quite bleak. I honestly can't imagine a way out of the technological frame and I am simply not seeing my generation in common spaces. Even my ability to meaningfully connect with my peers through conversation is deteriorating by product of the sheer scale of potential engagements one has at any moment. It is quite overwhelming and I am afraid there is no technological answer here.

XorNot 2026/04/10 06:44
但就像……为什么？

这基本上就是“不要使用你不喜欢的东西”，而我们这个时代的麻烦似乎是有很多人不能或不会这样做。

这在某种程度上是一个年龄问题，但也绝对是各个世代的很多人都没有学会这一点：你可以停止使用东西。走开后突然发现你永远不想回头，即使回头也完全没有吸引力。

But like...why?

This is basically "just don't use things you don't enjoy" and the trouble of our time seems to be the number of people who can't or won't do that.

It's somewhat an age thing but also definitely a lot of people in all generations never learn it: you can just stop using things. Walk away and suddenly find you never want to look back, and if you do it's entirely unappealing.

embedding-shape 2026/04/10 04:07

在 HN 中查看
andrewaylett 2026/04/10 04:22
这是作者与英国之间的问题（以及英国的在线安全法案，该法案对主机提出了仅不在英国就无法避免的要求），而不是英国与作者之间的问题。

但尽管我不喜欢 OSA：如果您不受英国法律的约束，为什么您（网站作者）关心我们的政府对您网站的看法？ 他们又不能对你做任何事。

It's the author's problem with the UK (and the UK's Online Safety Act, which establishes requirements on hosts that can't be avoided by merely not being in the UK), rather than the UK's problem with the author.

But as much as I dislike the OSA: if you're not subject to UK law, why do you (website author) care what our government thinks of your website? It's not like they can do anything to you.

minebreaker 2026/04/10 07:12
他有一篇文章是关于这个的

https://aphyr.com/posts/379-geoblocking-the-uk-with-debian-n...

> 明确地说，这是一个糟糕的解决方案。 MaxMind 的免费数据库并不是特别精确，而且一般来说 IP 查找表都在追踪移动的目标。我知道一个事实，非英国国家（例如爱尔兰！）的一些人无意中被这些查找表屏蔽了。让这些人使用 Tor 或 VPN 很糟糕，但我不知道在当前的监管环境下还能做什么。

He has an article for that

https://aphyr.com/posts/379-geoblocking-the-uk-with-debian-n...

> This is, to be clear, a bad solution. MaxMind’s free database is not particularly precise, and in general IP lookup tables are chasing a moving target. I know for a fact that there are people in non-UK countries (like Ireland!) who have been inadvertently blocked by these lookup tables. Making those people use Tor or a VPN sucks, but I don’t know what else to do in the current regulatory environment.
